expectations.evidence: profiles.toml lines 9-11: `[default.env]` section contains both `AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID = "{{ .Env.AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID }}"` and `AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY = "{{ .Env.AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY }}"`.

expectations.text: 일요일 오전 3시 schedule이 설정됨 (Sun 03:00 또는 유사 표현)
expectations.evidence: profiles.toml line 34: `schedule = "Sun *-*-* 03:00:00"` uses systemd calendar syntax for every Sunday at 03:00.

profiles.toml line 36: `permission = "system"` is set in the [backup-weekly.schedule] block. The assertion names it 'schedule-permission' but in resticprofile TOML the field is `permission` within a schedule block, which is the correct equivalent.

expectations.text: run-after에 healthchecks.io URL이 포함된 curl 명령어가 있음
expectations.evidence: profiles.toml lines 25-27: `run-after` contains `curl -fsS -m 10 --retry 5 -o /dev/null https://hc-ping.com/${HC_UUID}`, which is a healthchecks.io success ping URL.

expectations.text: run-after-fail에 healthchecks.io fail URL이 포함된 curl 명령어가 있음
expectations.evidence: profiles.toml lines 29-31: `run-after-fail` contains `curl -fsS -m 10 --retry 5 -o /dev/null https://hc-ping.com/${HC_UUID}/fail`, which is the healthchecks.io failure endpoint.

The profiles.toml file uses correct TOML syntax: section headers in brackets, key = value pairs with proper quoting, nested sections ([default.env], [backup-weekly.backup], [backup-weekly.schedule]), and array values. No syntax errors are detectable.

claims.claim: AWS credentials are read from the shell environment at runtime using Go template syntax
claims.evidence: profiles.toml uses `{{ .Env.AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID }}` and `{{ .Env.AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY }}`, which is the correct resticprofile Go template syntax for reading environment variables.

claims.claim: The schedule uses systemd calendar syntax
claims.evidence: `schedule = "Sun *-*-* 03:00:00"` is valid systemd OnCalendar format specifying every Sunday at 03:00.

claims.claim: run-before is included to signal backup start
claims.evidence: profiles.toml lines 21-23 contain a `run-before` hook with the healthchecks.io `/start` endpoint. This was not required by the eval but is a valid addition.

claims.claim: permission = "system" installs systemd units as system-level services
claims.evidence: In resticprofile, `permission = "system"` within a schedule block directs resticprofile to install systemd units under /etc/systemd/system rather than the user session, which matches the stated intent.

suggestions.reason: The assertion uses the key name `schedule-permission` but the actual resticprofile TOML field within a [profile.schedule] block is `permission`. An output using `schedule-permission` at the top level of a backup block (which is incorrect syntax) would still pass this assertion since the check only looks for the string. Consider asserting that `permission = "system"` appears inside a schedule sub-block.

No assertion checks that the env block is placed in the correct scope (e.g., under [default] or the backup profile) so that it actually propagates to the restic process. An env block placed under [global] or outside any profile would satisfy current assertions but would not work correctly.

eval_feedback.overall: Assertions cover the main requirements well and the output fully satisfies all of them. Minor gaps exist around the exact key name for schedule permission and env block placement correctness.
